Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(2 comments)

Maybe I'm missing something, but isn't the parent FID already stored in the ".." entry of the directory stripe?  Then there is no need to do a remote getxattr for each changelog entry. 

However, there is still some compatibility concern with changing this entry, so it makes sense to have a tunable to enable/disable this option.  The main question is whether it should be on or off by default?  If on by default, it simplifies things for most users, but may break tools that expect the stripe FID to be returned.  If off by default it keeps compatibility but makes it harder to fix the situation in the long run.  

Another option would be to store the parent FID in a new record, like "ppfid=" that "aware" programs/tools can use, but it won't break old ones. 

I've added a few additional reviewers who are more familiar with HSM ChangeLog users to get their input on the best path forward.

Patch Set 1:

> Maybe I'm missing something, but isn't the parent FID already stored in the ".." entry of the directory stripe?  Then there is no need to do a remote getxattr for each changelog entry. 

Andreas, '..' as well as linkea parent link point to the parent, yes, however having striped parent, the parent is a shard, its fid is stored there. the problem case is a remote dir, when its parent is on another MDT and the parent may be striped, thus the fid points to just a shard.

Patch Set 1:

> However, there is still some compatibility concern with changing this entry, so it makes sense to have a tunable to enable/disable this option.  The main question is whether it should be on or off by default?  If on by default, it simplifies things for most users, but may break tools that expect the stripe FID to be returned.  If off by default it keeps compatibility but makes it harder to fix the situation in the long run.  

do you mean to start using '..' for ppfid? mkfs tunable? what to do with existent FS's?

also, we do not have ppfid in hand (in case of striped parent) for mkdir and others, do we? thus remote getxattr will be needed at the time of the op.

Patch Set 1:

> > However, there is still some compatibility concern with changing
 > > this entry, so it makes sense to have a tunable to enable/disable
 > > this option.  The main question is whether it should be on or off
 > > by default?  If on by default, it simplifies things for most users,
 > > but may break tools that expect the stripe FID to be returned.  If
 > > off by default it keeps compatibility but makes it harder to fix
 > > the situation in the long run.
 > 
 > do you mean to start using '..' for ppfid? mkfs tunable? what to do
 > with existent FS's?
 > 
 > also, we do not have ppfid in hand (in case of striped parent) for
 > mkdir and others, do we? thus remote getxattr will be needed at the
 > time of the op.

I'm thinking that the MDS changelog record is currently storing the parent directory *shard* FID (pfid), but I _think_ the shard directory itself stores the *visible* parent directory FID (ppfid) in the ".." entry so it should be possible to get the ppfid without an RPC except for the rare case of a file that has been renamed and is remote from the shard.

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 1:

> I'm thinking that the MDS changelog record is currently storing the parent directory *shard* FID (pfid), but I _think_ the shard directory itself stores the *visible* parent directory FID (ppfid) in the ".." entry so it should be possible to get the ppfid without an RPC except for the rare case of a file that has been renamed and is remote from the shard.

it works this way. mdd_changelog_ns_pfid_set() calls mdo_xattr_get(parent) which is local if parent (shard) is local, and mdd_parent_fid(parent) which reads linkEA/'..' in it, which is local as well if parent is local.

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 1:

Andreas, questions answered? or did I miss something?

Patch Set 1:

Vitaly,
I _thought_ I understood this, but now I'm unsure again.

My understanding is that "parent FID" in the changelog record *should* be the FID of the parent of a shard (striped directory master), which is stored in the ".." entry of each shard.  For example:

 # lfs mkdir -c4 /mnt/testfs/stripedir
 # mkdir /mnt/testfs/stripedir/d1
 # lfs path2fid /mnt/testfs/stripedir{,/d1}
 /mnt/testfs/stripedir: [0x200000404:0x6f:0x0]
 /mnt/testfs/stripedir/d1: [0x280000404:0x548:0x0]
 # lfs getdirstripe /mnt/testfs/stripedir
 lmv_stripe_count: 4 lmv_stripe_offset: 0 lmv_hash_type: fnv_1a_64
 mdtidx           FID[seq:oid:ver]
      0           [0x200000400:0x8:0x0]  
      1           [0x240000401:0x8:0x0]  
      2           [0x280000402:0x8:0x0]  
      3           [0x2c0000403:0x7:0x0] 
 # lfs changelog testfs-MDT0002
 1 02MKDIR 23:44:19.203706494 2023.07.27 0x0 t=[0x280000404:0x548:0x0] j=mkdir.0
     ef=0xf u=0:0 nid=0@lo p=[0x280000402:0x8:0x0] d1
 # debugfs -c -R "ls -lD REMOTE_PARENT_DIR/0x280000402:0x8:0x0" /dev/loop2
  25194   40755  (2)  0  0   4096 27-Jul-2023 17:44 .
  25001   40755 (18)  0  0  16384 27-Jul-2023 17:44 fid:[0x200000404:0x6f:0x0] ..
  25195   40755 (18)  0  0   4096 27-Jul-2023 17:44 fid:[0x280000404:0x548:0x0] d1

So this changelog record is incorrectly storing the shard FID "p=[0x280000402:0x8:0x0]" when it *should* be storing the FID of "stripedir", as "p=[0x200000404:0x6f:0x0]".  The parent FID is clearly available in the ".." entry of the shard, so no remote lookup should be needed.  So there is a bug in how the changelog record is recorded.

The internal structure of the directory shouldn't matter to the "mkdir 'd1' in FID of 'stripedir/' directory", but I don't see how a remote RPC is needed.  What am I missing here?


Other comments that would need a refresh on the patch in any case, but only once this is understood properly.

Unless the performance impact is *really* bad, I would prefer that this functionality be enabled by default, since it makes the changelogs "correct" and that is probably more important than a small percentage slowdown for a small percentage of directories.

Have you measured how much the performance impact is for this change (if we still need the remote RPC)?  Striped directories should be pretty uncommon (only really for huge single directories), especially since DNE3 space balancing is available to handle normal MDT balance.

If this is expensive, it would also make sense to cache the pfid into the shard object in memory, so it only needs to be fetched once in the object lifetime.  In most cases, it is common to create a *lot* of entries in a striped directory rapidly, so caching the ppfid would amortize the RPC cost over many changelog entries.  It would need to be invalidated on rename, but that is an uncommon operation and (IIRC) already has to touch the shard objects.

The other thing I requested in my earlier patch review is to rename this feature parameter to "enable_remote_pfid" as other feature "enable_foo" parameters are named.  The name of the tunable parameter should also be added at least in the patch commit message.  It would also be useful to describe this parameter in the lfs-changelog.1 man page so that users know that this can affect the behaviour of the changelog records they are processing.

Patch Set 1:

> but I don't see how a remote RPC is needed. 

suppose the parent is not striped, we do space balancing across our MDS’s, sub-dir appears to be remote, however the sub-dir does not know if it (sub-dir) was created as a remote in a striped dir or in not a striped dir. if it knew its parent is not striped, it would be enough to take the pfid and no RPC would be needed. however to confirm the parent is not a shard we need to send an RPC.

> Striped directories should be pretty uncommon

striped dirs (usually) have no problems as shard and sub-dirs are on the same MDS. the problem is remote objects because it is not clear if their parent is a shard or a not-striped dir. thus space balancing does create remote sub-dirs and this is pretty much common case as you said now.

> it would also make sense to cache the pfid into the shard object in memory, so it only needs to be fetched once in the object lifetime.

right, it would be helpful.

> Have you measured how much the performance impact is for this change

Nikitas will shed a light on it.

Patch Set 1:

> suppose the parent is not striped, we do space balancing across our
 > MDS’s, sub-dir appears to be remote, however the sub-dir does not
 > know if it (sub-dir) was created as a remote in a striped dir or in
 > not a striped dir. if it knew its parent is not striped, it would
 > be enough to take the pfid and no RPC would be needed. however to
 > confirm the parent is not a shard we need to send an RPC.

I'm surprised that there is no way to determine from the shard whether it is a shard or not?  Is there an lmv xattr on the shard or a flag in the LMA that can determine whether the directory is a shard or not?

 > > Striped directories should be pretty uncommon
 > 
 > striped dirs (usually) have no problems as shard and sub-dirs are
 > on the same MDS.

???   Only one shard is on the same MDT as the master stripe, and all others are remote...

 > the problem is remote objects because it is not
 > clear if their parent is a shard or a not-striped dir. thus space
 > balancing does create remote sub-dirs and this is pretty much
 > common case as you said now.

True, but then either there is some way to check from the trusted.lmv whether the directory is just remote or a shard, or we should set a flag on the remote directory if it is a shard.  That would not solve the problem for existing striped directories, but if the flag is present then we can avoid sending the RPC for newly created directories or set it on existing ones after the first RPC.

Patch Set 1:

> I'm surprised that there is no way to determine from the shard whether it is a shard or not?  Is there an lmv xattr on the shard or a flag in the LMA that can determine whether the directory is a shard or not?

the shard is already the parent of the dir we are having in hand. however all we know yet it is the remote one and no idea who is the parent - a shard or a not striped dir.

>  > striped dirs (usually) have no problems as shard and sub-dirs are
>  > on the same MDS.
> 
> ???   Only one shard is on the same MDT as the master stripe, and all others are remote...

no-no, there is (1) a master striped dir, (2) its shards and (3) sub-dirs in these shards. having a sub-dir in hand, consider a remote one (yes, a rare case - usually sub-dirs are local for shards - but it is still possible), we need to understand if its parent is a shard (yes, the shard will tell about itself it is the shard, no need to go to the master one) or its parent is a not striped dir.

> True, but then either there is some way to check from the trusted.lmv whether the directory is just remote or a shard, or we should set a flag on the remote directory if it is a shard.  That would not solve the problem for existing striped directories, but if the flag is present then we can avoid sending the RPC for newly created directories or set it on existing ones after the first RPC.

the same as above - the dir is a remote, but who is its parent?

Patch Set 1:

(5 comments)

Vitaly, thank you for the explanation.  I can see the issue now when there is a remote entry under a striped directory (I don't think it matters whether this is an actual sub-directory or a regular file.

Given that the overhead of an extra remote getxattr is high, it makes sense to optimize this.  the CRUSH2 hash should reduce the number of remote entries created in striped directories due to common renames (e.g. rsync), but this is only landed in v2_15_50-185-g1ac4b9598a.  Caching the real parent FID in the mdd_object would be relatively low cost, and is only needed for remote objects in striped directories.

Patch Set 1:

> Vitaly, thank you for the explanation.  I can see the issue now
 > when there is a remote entry under a striped directory (I don't
 > think it matters whether this is an actual sub-directory or a
 > regular file.

The explanation should be added to the commit message so that it is more clear why this is needed.

parents.subject: LU-16695 llite: switch to ki_flags from f_flags

commit.subject: LU-10283 mdd: fix parent FID in changelog of striped directory
commit.message:
LU-10283 mdd: fix parent FID in changelog of striped directory

Changelog entry for the file operations such as create, rename,
link, unlink, mkdir referred to parent FID ("p=") as a shard's
FID in a striped directory. The same was true for the source's
parent FID ("sp="). This commit hides the Lustre intrinsics from
user displaying the parent's directory FID instead as expected.

An object might be in a remote MDT, in which case obtaining the parent
FID via the linkEA can be an expensive operation, so the parent FID is
cached in the mdd_object, so that the cost of the cross-MDT RPC is
amortized over the lifetime of the object.

Certain userspace tools might depend on the previous behavior of
displaying the shard's parent FID in the changelog records, so this
canp be enabled by setting mdd.*.enable_shard_pfid=1, if this is
required for compatibility.

HPE-bug-id: LUS-10721
Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ivanov <dmitry.ivanov2@hpe.com>
Change-Id: Iae15b49f5852f36ba62ae1706d3a5f4ebf307bc4
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/51322
Tested-by: jenkins <devops@whamcloud.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <maloo@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Lai Siyao <lai.siyao@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <adilger@whamcloud.com>
